hook: South Africa's oldest surviving colonial building, a five-pointed Dutch East India Company stone fort raised on what was then the shoreline.
facts: Built by the Dutch East India Company (VOC) between 1666 and 1679, replacing Jan van Riebeeck's 1652 clay-and-timber fort.
The first stone was laid on 2 January 1666 and the castle was completed by April 1679.
Its pentagonal plan has five bastions named after titles of William III of Orange: Leerdam, Buuren, Katzenellenbogen, Nassau and Oranje.
Stone was cut from a granite outcrop on Signal Hill, with slate and shells brought across from Robben Island.
It is regarded as the oldest surviving colonial building in South Africa and now houses the Castle Military Museum and the William Fehr Collection.
w: South Africa's oldest surviving colonial building -- a five-pointed VOC stone fort raised 1666-79 on the old shoreline, its bastions named for the titles of William of Orange. The Dutch cut its granite from Signal Hill and ferried slate and shells from Robben Island. It guards a military museum now. The Cape's first hard architecture.

hook: The first mosque in South Africa, founded amid the painted houses of Bo-Kaap and tended by an exiled imam who wrote out the Quran from memory in prison.
facts: Completed in 1794 on Dorp Street, it is recognised as the first mosque established in South Africa.

It stands on land bought in 1794 by Coridon van Ceylon, a Vryezwarten (freed Black Muslim) and the first Muslim to own property in Cape Town; his daughter Saartjie van de Kaap donated the building for use as a mosque.
Its first imam was Qadi Abdussalam, known as Tuan Guru, a political exile who according to the mosque wrote out an entire Quran from memory while imprisoned; that manuscript is held at the mosque.
It was where Cape Muslim traditions and the Arabic-Afrikaans script were first taught, and remains a symbol of the freedom of formerly enslaved people to worship.
Major renovations were carried out in 1907 and 1936; the mosque marked its 230th year in 2024.
w: The first mosque in South Africa, founded in 1794 among the painted houses of Bo-Kaap on land a freed slave bought. Its first imam, the exiled Tuan Guru, is said to have written out the whole Quran from memory while jailed on Robben Island -- that manuscript is kept here. Where Cape Muslim worship, and the Arabic-Afrikaans script, began.

hook: A former mission church preserving the memory of the inner-city community that apartheid bulldozers erased.
facts: Housed in the former Methodist Mission Church at 25A Buitenkant Street, one of the few buildings in District Six left standing after demolition.
In 1966 the apartheid government declared District Six a white area under the Group Areas Act; more than 60,000 residents were forcibly removed over the following years.
The District Six Museum Foundation was established in 1989 and the museum opened in December 1994.
Its centrepiece is a long hand-written 'memory cloth' recording first-hand accounts of life in the district and the trauma of removal, alongside a floor map of vanished streets.
w: A former mission church that holds the memory of the neighbourhood apartheid bulldozed. In 1966 the regime declared District Six whites-only; over the years that followed, more than 60,000 people were forced out and their streets flattened. The museum's floor is a map of the vanished roads, walked over by those who lived on them.

hook: The VOC's 1652 vegetable plot that fed passing ships, now a shaded city park guarding the oldest cultivated pear tree in the country.
facts: The Dutch East India Company laid out the garden in 1652 to grow fresh produce for the settlement and passing ships; master gardener Hendrik Boom prepared the first ground for sowing on 29 April 1652.
It holds the oldest cultivated pear tree in South Africa (circa 1652), brought from Holland in van Riebeeck's time and still bearing fruit.
It is the oldest garden in South Africa and a national heritage site.
By 1658 nearly every garden plant of Europe and India was being cultivated there, and the colony became largely self-sustaining.
w: The Dutch East India Company's 1652 vegetable plot, dug to feed passing ships -- now a shaded city park, and the oldest garden in the country. Among its trees stands a pear brought from Holland in van Riebeeck's day, still bearing fruit after nearly four centuries. Squirrels, roses and a kitchen garden that grew into a colony.

hook: A pair of Georgian cannon that have boomed midday across the Mother City since 1806, audible from the streets of Bo-Kaap below.
facts: A time signal has been fired by gun in Cape Town daily since 1806.
The gun sits at Lion Battery on Signal Hill, moved to its present position in 1902 overlooking the harbour and Table Bay.
The two 18-pounder smoothbore muzzle-loaders are reportedly the oldest guns still in daily use in the world; they alternate, with one kept as backup.
The guns are loaded six days a week (not Sundays or public holidays), with one fired at exactly midday.
w: Two Georgian cannon that have boomed midday across the Mother City since 1806 -- reportedly the oldest guns still in daily use anywhere. They sit at Lion Battery on Signal Hill, loaded six mornings a week, one fired on the stroke of twelve while the other waits in reserve. The whole city checks its watch by the bang.

n: St George's Cathedral & the Arch for Arch
hook: Desmond Tutu's 'People's Cathedral', where he led the 1989 march against apartheid and where his ashes now rest beneath the high altar.
facts: Designed by Sir Herbert Baker, with the foundation stone laid in 1901; it is the oldest cathedral in southern Africa.
It is known as 'the People's Cathedral' for its role under Archbishop Desmond Tutu in the struggle against apartheid; in 1989 a mass protest march of tens of thousands set out from it.
Tutu's ashes were interred in front of the high altar under a memorial stone after his death in December 2021.

Outside, the 'Arch for Arch' (2017), designed by Snohetta with Local Studio, weaves 14 strands of bent wood representing the 14 chapters of South Africa's constitution, marking where Tutu began many protests.
w: Desmond Tutu's 'People's Cathedral', from which he led a march of tens of thousands against apartheid in 1989 -- and where his ashes now rest beneath the high altar. Herbert Baker laid its foundation in 1901. Outside, the Arch for Arch bends fourteen wooden strands for the fourteen chapters of the constitution. Faith that marched.

hook: A candy-striped tower on the Mouille Point seafront, the oldest working lighthouse in South Africa and a fog-horn neighbours have long grumbled about.
facts: First lit on 12 April 1824, it is the oldest operational lighthouse in South Africa.
Designed by German architect Herman Schutte, it was the first solid masonry lighthouse on the South African coast (built 1821-1823).
Its red-and-white diagonal bands were added in 1950 to distinguish the tower from surrounding cottages.
Originally lit by Argand lamps burning sperm-whale oil, visible for about 6 nautical miles; the range was raised to 22 nautical miles in 1922.
Though on the edge of Mouille Point it is officially the Green Point Lighthouse and is often misnamed the Mouille Point Lighthouse.
w: A candy-striped tower on the Mouille Point seafront, first lit in 1824 -- the oldest working lighthouse in South Africa, and a foghorn the neighbours have grumbled about for a century. Its red-and-white diagonal bands were painted on in 1950 to tell it from the cottages around it. Once it burned whale oil; now its beam reaches 22 miles.

hook: An ossuary built to rebury the thousands of slaves and colonial-era poor whose unmarked graves were dug up by a luxury-flats development.
facts: In 2003 construction on Prestwich Street uncovered the bones of thousands of people in an informal burial ground on the old edge of colonial Cape Town, dated by UCT archaeologists to roughly 180-270 years old.
The graves held enslaved people, executed prisoners, washerwomen, free Blacks and others of the historic Cape underclass, buried in VOC settlement times.
After protests over the exhumations, the remains were re-interred in 2008 at the new Prestwich Memorial on the corner of Somerset Road and Buitengracht Street.

The ossuary holds the remains of 343 full bodies plus other bones, and stands beside the city's first Presbyterian church, which after slavery's 1838 abolition was the first to admit freed slaves to worship.
w: An ossuary built to rebury the forgotten. In 2003 a block of luxury flats broke ground on Prestwich Street and turned up the bones of thousands -- slaves, prisoners, washerwomen, the free Black poor of the old Cape. After protests over the digging, 343 bodies were re-interred here in 2008. The underclass that built the city, reclaimed.

hook: A walled tidal pool on the Sea Point promenade built so an invalid wife could bathe unseen, reached by a private tunnel under the road.
facts: The pool was built by wine merchant Pieter Marais in 1910 so his paralysed wife could be bathed in the sea daily.
A tunnel ran from Marais' manor house, Bordeaux, under the public road to the pool so she could be carried to it unseen.
It takes its name from businessman and politician Jacobus Arnoldus Graaff, who later acquired it and bequeathed it to the City of Cape Town.
For most of its life it was a men-only nude bathing spot, walled on all but its seaward side; in the late 1980s to early 2000s it was a well-known gay meeting place.
The high walls were demolished after 2005 over public-order concerns; the pool remains a landmark on the Sea Point promenade.
legend: Local accounts disagree over whether the tunnel from the Bordeaux villa to the pool genuinely existed or is embellished folklore; press features describe it as a 'secret tunnel' whose reality is debated.
w: A walled tidal pool on the Sea Point promenade, built in 1910 by a wine merchant so his paralysed wife could bathe in the sea unseen -- reached, the story goes, by a private tunnel under the road from their villa. For decades after, men bathed here nude. The high walls came down in 2005; the pool stayed. Privacy, set in stone and surf.

hook: Since 1929 a cable car has hauled visitors up Table Mountain's sheer face — and since 1997 its floor rotates a full 360 degrees on the way.
facts: Opened on 4 October 1929 by the Mayor of Cape Town, A.J.S. Lewis; the original wood-and-steel cars carried about 19 people.
The current Swiss-built Rotair cars were installed by Garaventa (Doppelmayr Garaventa Group) in 1997 and rotate 360 degrees during the roughly 4-5 minute ascent.
Each modern car carries up to 65 passengers; the upper station sits about 1,067 m above sea level, a vertical rise of roughly 765 m.
The cableway was the brainchild of Norwegian engineer Trygve Stromsoe, with foundations laid in 1926.
Cars were upgraded in 1958, 1974 and 1997.
w: Since 1929 a cable car has hauled visitors up Table Mountain's sheer face -- and since 1997 the floor turns a full circle as it climbs, so every rider gets the whole view. The Swiss Rotair cars carry 65 at a time to the 1,067-metre top in about five minutes. A Norwegian engineer dreamed it up; the foundations went in by hand in 1926.

hook: The highest point of Table Mountain is marked by a black stone cairn built in 1844 to help measure the curvature of the Earth.
facts: At 1,086 m (3,563 ft) it is the highest point on Table Mountain, higher than the cableway's upper station.
The cairn was completed in December 1844 on the instructions of Astronomer Royal Sir Thomas Maclear.
It stands about 5 m high and was painted lamp-black to make it visible across distances.
It served as a triangulation station for Maclear's re-measurement of the Cape arc of the meridian, expanding on earlier work by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille.
w: The true summit of Table Mountain, higher than the cableway, marked by a black stone cairn five metres tall. Sir Thomas Maclear had it built in 1844 as a survey station to re-measure the curve of the Earth across the Cape, picking up where Lacaille left off. Painted lamp-black to be seen for miles. Science, cairned on a peak.

hook: The first botanical garden in the world devoted to a country's own indigenous flora, laid out on land Cecil Rhodes left to the nation — now circled by a snaking treetop walkway nicknamed the Boomslang.
facts: Cecil John Rhodes bought the Kirstenbosch estate in 1895 and bequeathed it to the government on his death in 1902; it was set aside as a national botanical garden on 1 July 1913.
It is widely described as the first botanical garden in the world established to grow a country's own indigenous plants.
The Centenary Tree Canopy Walkway, opened to the public on 17 May 2014, is informally called the Boomslang ('tree snake'); the curving timber-and-steel path runs about 130 m through the Arboretum.
Botanists Harold Pearson and Neville Pillans identified the neglected site as suitable for the garden.
w: The first botanical garden in the world planted for a country's own wild flora, laid out in 1913 on land Cecil Rhodes left to the nation. Among the fynbos winds the Boomslang -- a curving steel-and-timber walkway opened in 2014 that lifts you into the tree canopy. A garden of nothing but what grew here already.

hook: A granite Greek temple bolted to the flank of Devil's Peak, with eight bronze lions and 49 steps for the 49 years of Cecil Rhodes's life.
facts: Designed by Sir Herbert Baker and built of Cape granite quarried on Table Mountain, it was completed in 1912 as a memorial to Cecil John Rhodes (1853-1902).
Its staircase has 49 steps, one for each year of Rhodes's life, flanked by eight bronze lions by John Macallan Swan, modelled on the lions of Nelson's Column.
At the foot stands the bronze equestrian sculpture 'Physical Energy' by George Frederic Watts.
The popular tea room / restaurant behind the memorial was destroyed in the April 2021 Table Mountain fire and had not been rebuilt.
w: A granite Greek temple bolted to the flank of Devil's Peak, built in 1912 for Cecil Rhodes -- 49 steps for his 49 years, and eight bronze lions modelled on those at Nelson's Column. Herbert Baker cut its stone from the mountain itself. The famous tea room behind it burned in the 2021 fire. Empire, in temple form, gazing north.

hook: A 1796 Dutch tower windmill on the slopes of Devil's Peak — gutted by the 2021 wildfire, ground back to working order by 2024.
facts: Built around 1796 as a private mill on the farm 'Welgelegen', breaking the Dutch East India Company's milling monopoly.
It is a three-storey tower mill; it takes its name from later owner Sybrand Jacobus Mostert.
Restored by Dutch millwright Christiaan Bremer and reopened on 1 February 1936, with Prime Minister General Hertzog attending.
Gutted in the 18 April 2021 Table Mountain fire, leaving only the masonry tower; fully restored and grinding wheat again by April 2024.
w: A 1796 Dutch tower windmill on the slopes of Devil's Peak, raised to break the Company's grip on milling. Dutch millwrights restored it in 1936; then the 2021 wildfire swept through and gutted it to the bare masonry. By 2024 its sails turned again and it was grinding wheat. A survivor twice over, on a city hillside.

hook: Inside Groote Schuur Hospital, the very rooms where Christiaan Barnard performed the world's first human heart transplant in 1967, recreated with lifelike tableaux.
facts: The world's first successful human-to-human heart transplant was performed here by Professor Christiaan Barnard on 3 December 1967.
The heart of 25-year-old accident victim Denise Darvall was transplanted into 53-year-old Louis Washkansky, who survived 18 days before dying of pneumonia.
The museum opened on 3 December 2007, the 40th anniversary of the operation, in the Old Main Building's original theatres and wards.
It recreates the events of the day using lifelike static tableaux on guided tours.
w: The theatres in Groote Schuur Hospital where, on 3 December 1967, Christiaan Barnard sewed a young woman's heart into a dying man -- the world's first human heart transplant. Louis Washkansky lived eighteen days. The rooms are recreated with figures, frozen on the night medicine changed. Where a heart first crossed between two bodies.

hook: A cast-iron Victorian drinking fountain stranded on a traffic island, with troughs for horses, basins for dogs and a lantern said to be among Cape Town's first electric lights.
facts: Originally the Moodie Fountain, cast in iron in 1891 at Walter Macfarlane & Co.'s Saracen Foundry in Glasgow and presented to Rondebosch by George Pigot Moodie.
It has a circular horse-drinking trough on horses' legs, smaller ground basins for dogs, and a central post topped by a hexagonal lantern.
The lantern was first lit on 25 April 1892, initially powered by Moodie's own private power plant.
Declared a National Monument on 10 April 1964; it stands on a traffic island where Belmont Road meets Main Road, and was restored/replicated around 2020.
w: A cast-iron Victorian fountain stranded on a traffic island -- a trough for horses, low basins for dogs, and a lantern first lit in 1892, said to be among Cape Town's earliest electric lights. It was cast in Glasgow in 1891 and shipped out as a gift to Rondebosch. Now cars stream past a watering-place for a vanished kind of traffic.

hook: South Africa's oldest wine estate, granted to governor Simon van der Stel in 1685 — the cradle of the country's wine industry, with a gabled Cape Dutch manor and Cloete cellar.
facts: Land granted to VOC governor Simon van der Stel in 1685; wine has been produced here continuously ever since, making it South Africa's oldest wine estate.
The Cloete family acquired the property in 1779 and added the wine cellar in 1791, with the manor improved to designs associated with architect Louis Michel Thibault.
A fire in 1925 destroyed much of the manor; funds were raised to rebuild it in its Cape Dutch form.
It is a Provincial Heritage Site and operates as a living museum of the South African wine industry, administered in part by Iziko Museums.
w: South Africa's oldest wine estate, granted to governor Simon van der Stel in 1685 and making wine ever since -- the cradle of the whole Cape industry, gathered round a gabled Cape Dutch manor. The Cloetes added the cellar in 1791. Fire took much of the house in 1925; the country rebuilt it. A living museum you can still drink from.

hook: The estate behind Vin de Constance, the fabled sweet Constantia wine that Napoleon had shipped to his exile on St Helena and reputedly drank on his deathbed.
facts: The estate was split from Simon van der Stel's original Constantia grant; the sweet Constantia wine became one of the most coveted dessert wines in 18th- and 19th-century Europe.
Phylloxera in the late 19th century ended production and the original recipe was lost.
In the 1980s owner Duggie Jooste and winemaker Ross Gower revived the wine as Vin de Constance from late-harvest Muscat de Frontignan grapes.
Historical admirers of the original Constantia wine include Napoleon Bonaparte, Frederick the Great and Jane Austen, who name-checked 'Constantia' in 'Sense and Sensibility'.
legend: Napoleon, exiled on St Helena, is said to have had large quantities of Constantia shipped to him and, by repeated tradition, to have refused all else but a glass of it on his deathbed.
w: The estate behind Vin de Constance -- the fabled sweet wine Napoleon had shipped to his exile on St Helena and, by tradition, asked for on his deathbed. Phylloxera killed the vines and lost the recipe in the 1800s; the owners revived it from late-harvest Muscat in the 1980s. Jane Austen name-checked it. A legend, rebottled.

hook: A land-based colony of endangered African penguins that waddles among the granite boulders and back gardens of a Simon's Town beach.
facts: The colony began in 1983 when a breeding pair of African penguins settled on Foxy Beach near Boulders.
Numbers peaked at around 3,900 birds in 2005 before declining; it is one of only a handful of land-based African penguin colonies in the world.
It forms part of Table Mountain National Park and is managed by SANParks, which built raised boardwalks to protect the birds.
The African penguin (Spheniscus demersus) is classified as endangered.
w: A colony of endangered African penguins that nests among giant granite boulders -- and in the back gardens -- of a Simon's Town beach. It began with a single breeding pair in 1983 and swelled to nearly 4,000 birds by 2005. Boardwalks now keep the crowds off the nests. One of the few places on earth you share the sand with penguins.

hook: A bronze Great Dane on the harbour overlooks Jubilee Square — the only dog ever officially enlisted in the Royal Navy.
facts: Just Nuisance was a Great Dane born 1 April 1937 in Rondebosch and enlisted as an Able Seaman in the Royal Navy on 25 August 1939.
He was enlisted partly so he could travel free on trains between Simon's Town and Cape Town to escort sailors home.
He died on 1 April 1944 at Simon's Town Naval Hospital and was buried with full naval honours.
The bronze statue in Jubilee Square, sculpted by Jean Doyle, was erected in 1985.
w: A bronze Great Dane stands over Simon's Town's Jubilee Square -- the only dog ever formally enlisted in the Royal Navy. They signed him on as an Able Seaman in 1939, partly so he could ride the trains free while escorting sailors home from Cape Town. He died in 1944 and was buried with full naval honours. A ship's dog, cast in bronze.

hook: A whitewashed Dutch East India Company signal post from 1673 that has been a brothel, an ale house and a police station — among the oldest buildings in South Africa.
facts: Het Posthuys was built in 1673 by the Dutch East India Company as a signal station and military observation post, a year before the Castle of Good Hope was occupied.
Over the centuries it served as a toll-house, ale house, stable, barracks, brothel and police station.
It was restored in 1982-83 with support from the Anglo American Corporation and now operates as a museum.
It is managed by the Muizenberg Historical Conservation Society and sits on Muizenberg's 'Historical Mile' along Main Road.
w: A whitewashed Company signal post from 1673 -- older than the occupied Castle -- that has since been a toll-house, an ale house, a barracks, a brothel and a police station. It watched False Bay for the VOC, was restored in the 1980s, and now keeps a small museum on Muizenberg's Historical Mile. One little building that played every part.

hook: The modest seaside cottage where empire-builder Cecil John Rhodes died in 1902, now a small museum on Muizenberg's Main Road.
facts: Cecil John Rhodes acquired the cottage in 1899 and died there on 26 March 1902.
His reported last words were 'So little done, so much to do.'
The cottage is at 246 Main Road, Muizenberg, on the village's 'Historical Mile'.
It is run as a museum by the Muizenberg Historical Conservation Society.
w: The modest seaside cottage where Cecil Rhodes -- magnate, empire-builder, prime minister -- died in 1902, far from the grandeur he commanded. 'So little done, so much to do,' he is said to have murmured at the end. He'd bought the house on Muizenberg's Main Road only three years before. A small room for a vast, ruinous ambition.

n: Kalk Bay Harbour & the Brass Bell
hook: A working fishing harbour where the day's catch is auctioned off the quay, beside a seafood institution wedged between the railway line and the surf.
facts: Construction of Kalk Bay harbour began in 1913 and was largely completed by 1918, creating a basin with a breakwater and fish-landing quay.
By the late 1920s Kalk Bay supplied most of Cape Town's domestic fish market; it remains a working fishing harbour today.
The Brass Bell restaurant has operated beside Kalk Bay station since 1939, beginning as a council-run tea room.
The Cape Town to Simon's Town railway, opened to Kalk Bay in 1883, runs right alongside the harbour.
w: A working fishing harbour where the day's catch is still auctioned straight off the quay, hemmed between railway and surf. The basin was built between 1913 and 1918; by the 1920s it supplied most of Cape Town's fish. Wedged beside the station, the Brass Bell has fried that catch since 1939. Trains, tides and snoek, all in one cove.

n: Cape Point Lighthouse & Flying Dutchman Funicular
hook: A clifftop 1859 lighthouse reached by Africa's first commercial funicular, at the storm-lashed headland that gave the world the legend of a ghost ship.
facts: The original Cape Point lighthouse was built in 1859 near the summit of the headland, around 238 m above the sea.
Because the high lighthouse was often shrouded in cloud and missed by ships, a lower replacement light was later built; the old tower remains.
The Flying Dutchman Funicular, opened in 1996, is regarded as the first commercial funicular in Africa and carries visitors up to near the old lighthouse.
Cape Point lies within the Cape of Good Hope section of Table Mountain National Park.
legend: The Flying Dutchman is a phantom ship said to be doomed to sail the seas forever; folklore ties the tale to a Dutch vessel that foundered off the Cape around 1641 under a captain often named Hendrik van der Decken.
Sailors who heeded the spectral crew's pleas to carry letters home were said to be cursed themselves.
w: A clifftop lighthouse of 1859 at the storm-lashed headland that gave the world its most famous ghost ship -- reached now by the Flying Dutchman, said to be Africa's first commercial funicular. The high old light was so often lost in cloud that ships missed it, so a lower one was built; the original tower still stands.

hook: The rusting iron skeleton of a steamship that ran full-tilt onto Noordhoek's Long Beach in 1900 after her officer mistook Chapman's Peak for Cape Point.
facts: The Kakapo was a 1,093-ton steamship built in Scotland in 1898, named after a New Zealand parrot.
On 25 May 1900, during her maiden voyage from Swansea to Sydney, she ran aground on Noordhoek Beach in mist and heavy swell.
The officer of the watch mistook Chapman's Peak for Cape Point and turned to port, driving the ship high onto the sand; all 20 crew survived.
Salvage attempts failed and the wreck remains visible above the high-tide line on Long Beach.
w: The rusting iron skeleton of a steamship beached on Noordhoek's Long Beach since 1900 -- run aground on her maiden voyage when the officer of the watch mistook Chapman's Peak for Cape Point and turned too soon. She drove so high onto the sand that salvage failed; all twenty crew walked off alive. A Scottish ship, marooned on a Cape beach.

n: Mariner's Wharf & the Republic of Hout Bay
hook: Africa's first harbourfront emporium, in a fishing village that once cheekily declared itself an independent republic with its own passports.
facts: Mariner's Wharf was built in 1979 by Stanley Dorman on Hout Bay harbour and its fishmarket was officially opened on 22 November 1984, becoming Africa's first harbourfront emporium.
Hout Bay ('Houtbaai', meaning Wood Bay) was named by Dutch settlers whose wooded valley supplied timber for ships and the Castle of Good Hope.
The fishing village dates to around 1867, when German immigrant Jacob Trautmann began farming and fishing there.
In the 1980s residents jokingly declared Hout Bay a 'republic', issuing tongue-in-cheek passports as a tourism gimmick that genuinely drew visitors.
w: Africa's first harbourfront emporium, opened in 1984 on Hout Bay's working harbour -- in a fishing village that once half-jokingly declared itself a republic and issued its own tongue-in-cheek passports. The Dutch named the bay for the timber its valley sent to build ships and the Castle. A wharf with a flag and a punchline.

hook: A row of candy-coloured Victorian bathing boxes at Surfer's Corner, descendants of the wheeled 'bathing machines' that preserved Edwardian modesty.
facts: The huts descend from the early-1900s bathing boxes, themselves rooted in the 19th-century 'bathing machine' that let bathers change in privacy.
Surfer's Corner is regarded as the birthplace of surfing in South Africa, with surfing recorded at Muizenberg for over a century.
One of the earliest known photographs of a woman on a surfboard was taken at Muizenberg in the 1920s.
After plans to remove the huts sparked public outcry in 2017, the City of Cape Town allocated around R3 million for renovations at Surfer's Corner from 2022.
w: A row of candy-coloured bathing boxes at Surfer's Corner, the bright descendants of the wheeled Victorian 'bathing machines' that once let swimmers undress in modesty. This is where South Africa learned to surf -- the sport recorded here for over a century. The most photographed wood on the Cape coast.

hook: Seven granite figures on a busy Cape Flats roadside mark where police gunned down seven young anti-apartheid activists in 1986.
facts: On 3 March 1986, seven young men were killed by South African police in Gugulethu; they became known as the Gugulethu Seven.
The dead were Mandla Simon Mxinwa, Zanisile Zenith Mjobo, Zola Alfred Swelani, Godfrey Jabulani Miya, Christopher Piet, Themba Mlifi and Zabonke John Konile.
The memorial of seven granite figures, by artists Donovan Ward and Paul Hendricks, stands on NY1 near the killing site.
The monument was unveiled on 21 March 2005, South Africa's Human Rights Day.
w: Seven granite figures on a Cape Flats roadside mark where, on 3 March 1986, police gunned down seven young anti-apartheid activists. Their names are set into the monument -- Mxinwa, Mjobo, Swelani, Miya, Piet, Mlifi, Konile. Unveiled on Human Rights Day 2005, it stands on NY1 near the spot they fell. Seven who did not come home.
